14 January 2015 – 22 September 2023 | | | **Life tenure** 23 September 2005 – 15 May 2006 | | | Appointed by | Carlo Azeglio Ciampi | | [Member of the European Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_the_European_Parliament "Member of the European Parliament") | | | **In office** 20 July 1999 – 19 July 2004 | | | Constituency | [Southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy_(European_Parliament_constituency) "Southern Italy (European Parliament constituency)") | | **In office** 25 July 1989 – 10 June 1992 | | | Constituency | Southern Italy | | Member of the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_(Italy) "Chamber of Deputies (Italy)") | | | **In office** 5 June 1968 – 8 May 1996 | | | Constituency | [Naples](/wiki/Naples%E2%80%93Caserta_constituency "Naples–Caserta constituency") | | **In office** 25 June 1953 – 15 May 1963 | | | Constituency | Naples | | |

(Italian: [[ˈdʒordʒo napoliˈtaːno]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); 29 June 1925 – 22 September 2023) was an Italian politician who served as the [president of Italy](/wiki/President_of_Italy "President of Italy") from 2006 to 2015. At the time the longest-serving president in Italian history, and the first to achieve re-election,[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) he played a dominant role in [Italian politics](/wiki/Italian_politics "Italian politics") for almost a decade, leading some critics to refer to him as *Re Giorgio* ("King Giorgio").[[3]](#cite_note-Donadio_2011-3)

**Giorgio Napolitano** (Italian: [[ˈdʒordʒo napoliˈtaːno]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); 29 June 1925 – 22 September 2023) was an Italian politician who served as the [president of Italy](/wiki/President_of_Italy "President of Italy") from 2006 to 2015. At the time the longest-serving president in Italian history, and the first to achieve re-election,[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) he played a dominant role in [Italian politics](/wiki/Italian_politics "Italian politics") for almost a decade, leading some critics to refer to him as *Re Giorgio* ("King Giorgio").[[3]](#cite_note-Donadio_2011-3)

Napolitano was a longtime member of the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party"), which he joined in 1945 after taking part in the [Italian resistance movement](/wiki/Italian_resistance_movement "Italian resistance movement"), and of its post-Communist [democratic socialist](/wiki/Democratic_socialist "Democratic socialist") and [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democratic "Social democratic") successors, from the [Democratic Party of the Left](/wiki/Democratic_Party_of_the_Left "Democratic Party of the Left") to the [Democrats of the Left](/wiki/Democrats_of_the_Left "Democrats of the Left"). He was a leading member of *[migliorismo](/wiki/Migliorismo "Migliorismo")*, a [reformist](/wiki/Reformist "Reformist"), moderate, and modernizing faction on the right wing of the PCI,[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) which was inspired by the values of democratic socialism,[[7]](#cite_note-7) looked favourably to social democracy, and was interested in [revisionist Marxism](/wiki/Revisionist_Marxism "Revisionist Marxism").[[8]](#cite_note-8) First elected to the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_(Italy) "Chamber of Deputies (Italy)") in 1953, he took an assiduous interest in parliamentary life and was [president of the Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/President_of_the_Chamber_of_Deputies_(Italy) "President of the Chamber of Deputies (Italy)") from 1992 to 1994. He was [Minister of the Interior](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Interior_(Italy) "Minister of the Interior (Italy)") from 1996 to 1998 during the [first Prodi government](/wiki/First_Prodi_government "First Prodi government").[[9]](#cite_note-Scherer_2023-9) A close friend of [Henry Kissinger](/wiki/Henry_Kissinger "Henry Kissinger"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) he was also the first high-ranking leader of a [communist party](/wiki/Communist_party "Communist party") to visit the United States, which he did in 1978.[[9]](#cite_note-Scherer_2023-9)

In 2005, Napolitano was appointed a [senator for life in Italy](/wiki/Senator_for_life_in_Italy "Senator for life in Italy") by then president [Carlo Azeglio Ciampi](/wiki/Carlo_Azeglio_Ciampi "Carlo Azeglio Ciampi").[[11]](#cite_note-D'Emilio_2023-11) In the [May 2006 Italian presidential election](/wiki/May_2006_Italian_presidential_election "May 2006 Italian presidential election"), he was elected by the [Italian Parliament](/wiki/Italian_Parliament "Italian Parliament") as president of Italy. A [pro-Europeanist](/wiki/Pro-Europeanist "Pro-Europeanist"),[[12]](#cite_note-Vecchio_2023-12) Napolitano was the first former Communist to hold said office.[[11]](#cite_note-D'Emilio_2023-11) During his first term in office, he oversaw governments both of the [centre-left coalition](/wiki/Centre-left_coalition_(Italy) "Centre-left coalition (Italy)"), such as the [second Prodi government](/wiki/Second_Prodi_government "Second Prodi government"), and the [centre-right coalition](/wiki/Centre-right_coalition_(Italy) "Centre-right coalition (Italy)"), such as the [fourth Berlusconi government](/wiki/Fourth_Berlusconi_government "Fourth Berlusconi government").[[13]](#cite_note-13) In November 2011, [Silvio Berlusconi](/wiki/Silvio_Berlusconi "Silvio Berlusconi") resigned as [prime minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_Italy "Prime minister of Italy") amid financial and economic problems. In keeping with his constitutional role, Napolitano then asked former European commissioner [Mario Monti](/wiki/Mario_Monti "Mario Monti") to form a cabinet,[[14]](#cite_note-14) which critics referred to as a "government of the president".[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Napolitano intended to retire from politics after his seven-year presidential term expired, but reluctantly agreed to run again in the [2013 presidential election](/wiki/2013_Italian_presidential_election "2013 Italian presidential election") to safeguard the continuity of the country's institutions during the parliamentary deadlock that followed the [February 2013 Italian general election](/wiki/February_2013_Italian_general_election "February 2013 Italian general election"). He was the first sitting president to run for a second term.[[16]](#cite_note-Mackenzie_2013-16) On being re-elected as president with broad cross-party support in Parliament, he overcame the impasse by inviting [Enrico Letta](/wiki/Enrico_Letta "Enrico Letta") to propose a [grand coalition](/wiki/Grand_coalition "Grand coalition") government.[[17]](#cite_note-17) When Letta handed in his resignation in February 2014, Napolitano mandated [Matteo Renzi](/wiki/Matteo_Renzi "Matteo Renzi") (Letta's factional challenger) to form a new government.[[18]](#cite_note-18) After a record eight and a half years as president, citing age factors, the 89-year-old Napolitano resigned in January 2015. He had already stated that he did not intend to serve out a full second term.[[19]](#cite_note-19) He then resumed his [Italian Senate](/wiki/Italian_Senate "Italian Senate") seat,[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) which he held until his death in 2023.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Napolitano was often accused by his critics of having transformed a largely ceremonial role into a political and executive one, acting as kingmaker during his political tenure.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) Supporters instead credited him with saving Italy from the brink of default during the [European debt crisis](/wiki/European_debt_crisis "European debt crisis") and subsequent political stalemates,[[25]](#cite_note-25) which helped to stabilize the country.[[26]](#cite_note-McFadden_2023-26) At the time of his death in 2023, he was the longest-serving Italian President as well as the longest-lived Italian President on record.[[27]](#cite_note-Bincher_2023-27) He was also the oldest head of state in Europe and the third oldest in the world, behind the [Zimbabwean](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe") president [Robert Mugabe](/wiki/Robert_Mugabe "Robert Mugabe") and [Abdullah of Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Abdullah_of_Saudi_Arabia "Abdullah of Saudi Arabia").[[28]](#cite_note-Martelli_2023-28) A [state funeral](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Giorgio_Napolitano "Death and state funeral of Giorgio Napolitano") in secular form was held for Napolitano on 22 September 2023. In office for 8 years and 244 days, he was the longest-serving president, until the record was surpassed by [Sergio Mattarella](/wiki/Sergio_Mattarella "Sergio Mattarella") in 2023. He also was the longest-lived president in the [history of the Italian Republic](/wiki/History_of_the_Italian_Republic "History of the Italian Republic"),[[27]](#cite_note-Bincher_2023-27) which has been in existence since 1946. Although he was a prominent figure of the [First Italian Republic](/wiki/First_Italian_Republic "First Italian Republic"), he did not take part in the [Constituent Assembly of Italy](/wiki/Constituent_Assembly_of_Italy "Constituent Assembly of Italy") that drafted the [Italian constitution](/wiki/Italian_constitution "Italian constitution");[[27]](#cite_note-Bincher_2023-27) he is considered one of the symbols of the [Second Italian Republic](/wiki/Second_Italian_Republic "Second Italian Republic"), which came about after the *[Tangentopoli](/wiki/Tangentopoli "Tangentopoli")* scandal of the 1990s.[[27]](#cite_note-Bincher_2023-27)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Napolitano was born in [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"), [Campania](/wiki/Campania "Campania"), on 29 June 1925. His father, Giovanni, was a liberal lawyer and poet. His mother, Carolina (Bobbio), a descended from a noble [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont") family. Napolitano studied at the Classical Lyceum Umberto I, graduating in 1941. That year, his family moved to [Padua](/wiki/Padua "Padua") and he was graduated to the lyceum [Titus Livius](/wiki/Titus_Livius "Titus Livius"). In 1942, he matriculated at the [University of Naples Federico II](/wiki/University_of_Naples_Federico_II "University of Naples Federico II"), studying law. During this period, Napolitano adhered to the local [Fascist University Groups](/wiki/Fascist_University_Groups "Fascist University Groups") (*Gruppi Universitari Fascisti*), where he met his core group of friends, who shared his opposition to [Italian fascism](/wiki/Italian_fascism "Italian fascism").[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) As he would later state, the group "was in fact a true breeding ground of [anti-fascist](/wiki/Anti-fascist "Anti-fascist") intellectual energies, disguised and to a certain extent tolerated".[[31]](#cite_note-Napolitano_2005-31)

An enthusiast of the theatre since secondary school, during his university years he contributed a theatrical review to the *IX Maggio* weekly magazine and had small parts in plays organized by the *Gioventù Universitaria Fascista* itself. He played in a comedy by [Salvatore Di Giacomo](/wiki/Salvatore_Di_Giacomo "Salvatore Di Giacomo") at [Teatro Mercadante](/wiki/Teatro_Mercadante "Teatro Mercadante") in Naples. Napolitano dreamt of being an actor and spent his early years performing in several productions at the Teatro Mercadante.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) He also loved reading the poetry of [Giuseppe Ungaretti](/wiki/Giuseppe_Ungaretti "Giuseppe Ungaretti"), [Eugenio Montale](/wiki/Eugenio_Montale "Eugenio Montale"), and [Salvatore Quasimodo](/wiki/Salvatore_Quasimodo "Salvatore Quasimodo").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Napolitano has often been cited as the author of a collection of [sonnets](/wiki/Sonnet "Sonnet") in [Neapolitan dialect](/wiki/Neapolitan_dialect "Neapolitan dialect") published under a pseudonym, Tommaso Pignatelli, and entitled *Pe cupià 'o chiarfo* ("To Mimic the Downpour"). He denied this in 1997 and on the occasion of his presidential election, when his staff described the attribution of authorship to Napolitano as a "journalistic myth".[[36]](#cite_note-36) He published his first acknowledged book, entitled *Movimento Operaio e Industria di Stato* ("Workers' Movement and State Industry"), in 1962.[[37]](#cite_note-Quirinale.it-37)

## Early political career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early political career")]

### World War II and anti-fascist resistance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: World War II and anti-fascist resistance")]

During the existence of the [Italian Social Republic](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic") (1943–1945), a puppet state of [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") in the final period of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Napolitano and his circle of friends took part in several actions of the [Italian resistance movement](/wiki/Italian_resistance_movement "Italian resistance movement") against German and Italian [fascist](/wiki/Fascist "Fascist") forces.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) In 1944, along with the group of Neapolitan [communists](/wiki/Communists "Communists"), such as [Mario Palermo](/w/index.php?title=Mario_Palermo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mario Palermo (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mario_Palermo "it:Mario Palermo")] and [Maurizio Valenzi](/wiki/Maurizio_Valenzi "Maurizio Valenzi"), Napolitano prepared the arrival in Naples of [Palmiro Togliatti](/wiki/Palmiro_Togliatti "Palmiro Togliatti"),[[40]](#cite_note-40) the long-time leader of the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") (PCI) who was in exile since 1926 when the [Communist Party of Italy](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Italy "Communist Party of Italy") (PCd'I) was banned by the [Fascist regime in Italy](/wiki/Fascist_regime_in_Italy "Fascist regime in Italy"); Togliatti was one of few leaders not to be arrested, as he was attending a meeting of the [Comintern](/wiki/Comintern "Comintern") in Moscow.[[41]](#cite_note-41) In 1945, Napolitano joined the PCI.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### From post-war years to the Hungarian Revolution of 1956

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: From post-war years to the Hungarian Revolution of 1956")]

Following the end of the war in 1945, Napolitano became the PCI's federal secretary for Naples and [Caserta](/wiki/Caserta "Caserta"). In 1947, he graduated in [jurisprudence](/wiki/Jurisprudence "Jurisprudence") with a final dissertation on political economy, entitled *Il mancato sviluppo industriale del Mezzogiorno dopo l'unità e la legge speciale per Napoli del 1904* ("The Lack of Industrial Development in the [Mezzogiorno](/wiki/Mezzogiorno "Mezzogiorno") following the [Unification of Italy](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy") and the Special Law of 1904 for Naples").[[44]](#cite_note-off-bio-44) He became a member of the Secretariat of the Italian Economic Centre for Southern Italy in 1946, which was represented by [Giuseppe Paratore](/wiki/Giuseppe_Paratore "Giuseppe Paratore"), where he remained for two years. Napolitano played a major role in the Movement for the Rebirth of Southern Italy for over ten years.[[37]](#cite_note-Quirinale.it-37)

On 11 June 1946, nine days after the [1946 Italian institutional referendum](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum") in which Italy became a republic, Napolitano was at the headquarters of the Communist federation in via Medina when they were besieged for many hours by a crowd of royalist demonstrators who were enraged by the display of the party's red flag and the tricolor without the Savoy coat of arms.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Many of them were empoverished people who, in the words of Napolitano years later, were "immersed in the context of a real social disintegration".[[46]](#cite_note-Ranieri_2023-46) They were animated by feelings of loyalty to the [Savoy monarchy](/wiki/Savoy_monarchy "Savoy monarchy") and by the ancient feeling of [Southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy") separatism, which had reawakened against [Northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy"), which had first wanted fascism and had imposed it on the South, and now wanted to impose the republic. About this event, which became known as the [via Medina massacre](/w/index.php?title=Via_Medina_massacre&action=edit&redlink=1 "Via Medina massacre (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strage_di_via_Medina "it:Strage di via Medina")], he later ruled out that it was only "the work of provocateurs, troublemakers, professional agitators, and criminals".[[46]](#cite_note-Ranieri_2023-46)

In the [1953 Italian general election](/wiki/1953_Italian_general_election "1953 Italian general election"), Napolitano was first elected to the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_(Italy) "Chamber of Deputies (Italy)") for the electoral district of [Naples](/wiki/Naples%E2%80%93Caserta_constituency "Naples–Caserta constituency"). Apart from the [1963 Italian general election](/wiki/1963_Italian_general_election "1963 Italian general election"), when he did not seek re-election because he was the secretary of the Naples federation, a position he held from 1962 to 1966, he was always re-elected up to the [1996 Italian general election](/wiki/1996_Italian_general_election "1996 Italian general election").[[44]](#cite_note-off-bio-44) He was elected to the National Committee of the party during its eighth national congress in 1956, largely thanks to the support offered by Togliatti, who wanted to involve younger politicians in the central direction of the party. He became responsible for the commission for Southern Italy within the National Committee.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

A 26 May 1953 document of the [Italian Ministry of the Interior](/wiki/Italian_Ministry_of_the_Interior "Italian Ministry of the Interior") reported Napolitano as a member of the secret armed paramilitary groups of the PCI in the city of Rome, what became known as *Gladio Rossa*.[[48]](#cite_note-48) When the [Hungarian Revolution of 1956](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956 "Hungarian Revolution of 1956") and its military suppression by the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") (USSR) occurred, the leadership of the PCI labelled the insurgents as counter-revolutionaries, and the official party newspaper *[L'Unità](/wiki/L%27Unit%C3%A0 "L'Unità")* referred to them as "thugs" and "despicable agents provocateurs". Napolitano complied with the party-sponsored position on this matter, a choice for which he was later criticized;[[49]](#cite_note-49) he repeatedly declared to have become uncomfortable with the decision, developing what his autobiography describes as a "grievous self-critical torment". He would reason that his compliance was motivated by concerns about the role of the PCI as "inseparable from the fates of the socialist forces guided by the USSR" as opposed to imperialist forces.[[31]](#cite_note-Napolitano_2005-31)

The decision to support the Soviets against the Hungarian revolutionaries generated a split in the PCI, and the [CGIL](/wiki/CGIL "CGIL") (Italy's largest trade union, then supportive of the PCI) refused to conform to the party-sponsored position and applauded the revolution, on the basis that the eighth national congress of the PCI had stated that the "Italian Way to Socialism" was to be democratic and specific to the nation. These views were supported in the party by [Giorgio Amendola](/wiki/Giorgio_Amendola "Giorgio Amendola"), whom Napolitano would always look up to as a teacher. Frequently seen together, Amendola and Napolitano would jokingly be referred to by friends as respectively *Giorgio 'o chiatto* and *Giorgio 'o sicco* ("Giorgio the Pudgy" and "Giorgio the Slim" in the Neapolitan dialect).[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Berlinguer_Napolitano.jpg/250px-Berlinguer_Napolitano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Berlinguer_Napolitano.jpg)

Napolitano with [Enrico Berlinguer](/wiki/Enrico_Berlinguer "Enrico Berlinguer")

Between 1963 and 1966, Napolitano was party chairman in the city of Naples and later, between 1966 and 1969, he was appointed as chairman of the secretary's office and of the political office. In 1964, following the death of Togliatti, Napolitano was one of the main leaders who supported an alliance with the [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party"), which after the end of the [Popular Democratic Front](/wiki/Popular_Democratic_Front_(Italy) "Popular Democratic Front (Italy)") joined the government with [Christian Democracy](/wiki/Christian_Democracy_(Italy) "Christian Democracy (Italy)"). During the 1970s and 1980s, Napolitano was in charge for cultural activities, [economic policy](/wiki/Economic_policy "Economic policy"), and the [international relations](/wiki/International_relations "International relations") of the party.[[26]](#cite_note-McFadden_2023-26)

Napolitano's political thought was somewhat moderate in the context of the PCI; he became the leader of the wing of the party called *[migliorismo](/wiki/Migliorismo "Migliorismo")*, whose members notably included [Gerardo Chiaromonte](/wiki/Gerardo_Chiaromonte "Gerardo Chiaromonte") and [Emanuele Macaluso](/wiki/Emanuele_Macaluso "Emanuele Macaluso"). The term *migliorista* (from *migliore*, Italian for "better") was coined with a slightly mocking intent.[[52]](#cite_note-52) To be a *betterist* was regarded more negatively than to be a *reformist* by traditional Communists. Their aim was to reform and improve, hence the name, capitalism by [gradualist](/wiki/Gradualism_(politics) "Gradualism (politics)") means.[[28]](#cite_note-Martelli_2023-28) [Henry Kissinger](/wiki/Henry_Kissinger "Henry Kissinger") is said to have called Napolitano his "favourite Communist",[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) while *[La Stampa](/wiki/La_Stampa "La Stampa")* described him as an atypical Communist,[[55]](#cite_note-55) and once called him "the least communist Communist [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*] that the party ever enlisted".[[56]](#cite_note-56) In 2015, Kissinger presented him the [Henry A. Kissinger Prize](/wiki/Henry_A._Kissinger_Prize "Henry A. Kissinger Prize"), which is awarded by the [American Academy in Berlin](/wiki/American_Academy_in_Berlin "American Academy in Berlin") for exceptional contributions to transatlantic relations.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

In the mid-1970s, Napolitano was invited by the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology") to give a lecture; the United States ambassador to Italy, [John A. Volpe](/wiki/John_A._Volpe "John A. Volpe"), refused to grant Napolitano a [visa](/wiki/Visa_(document) "Visa (document)") on account of his membership of the PCI. Between 1977 and 1981, Napolitano had some secret meetings with the United States ambassador [Richard N. Gardner](/wiki/Richard_N._Gardner "Richard N. Gardner"), at a time when the PCI was seeking contact with the US administration, in the context of its definitive break with its past relationship with the [Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union") and the beginning of [Eurocommunism](/wiki/Eurocommunism "Eurocommunism"), the attempt to develop a theory and practice more adapted to the democratic countries of Western Europe. He was an active member of the party until it ended in 1991.[[58]](#cite_note-mahalo.com-58) In 2006, when Napolitano was elected [president of the Italian Republic](/wiki/President_of_the_Italian_Republic "President of the Italian Republic"), Gardner stated to the [Associated Press Television News](/wiki/Associated_Press_Television_News "Associated Press Television News") that he considered Napolitano "a real statesman", "a true believer in democracy", and "a friend of the United States [who] will carry out his office with impartiality and fairness".[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Thanks to this role and in part by the good offices of [Giulio Andreotti](/wiki/Giulio_Andreotti "Giulio Andreotti"), in the 1980s Napolitano was able to travel to the United States and give lectures at [Aspen, Colorado](/wiki/Aspen,_Colorado "Aspen, Colorado"), and at [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"). He has since visited and lectured in the United States several times. After the death of [Enrico Berlinguer](/wiki/Enrico_Berlinguer "Enrico Berlinguer") in 1984, Napolitano was among the possible successors as secretary of the party; [Alessandro Natta](/wiki/Alessandro_Natta "Alessandro Natta") was elected instead, and it was the second time that he came close to the party's highest position, the first time being in 1972 when Berlinguer was favoured over him.[[60]](#cite_note-60) In July 1989, Napolitano became foreign minister in the PCI shadow government, from which he resigned the day after the Congress of [Rimini](/wiki/Rimini "Rimini"), where the PCI was dissolved. That same year, Napolitano supported the motion that led to the PCI's transformation and name change.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

## After the Italian Communist Party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: After the Italian Communist Party")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Giorgio_Napolitano_daticamera.jpg/250px-Giorgio_Napolitano_daticamera.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giorgio_Napolitano_daticamera.jpg)

Napolitano in 1992

After the dissolution of the PCI in February 1991, Napolitano followed most of its membership into the [Democratic Party of the Left](/wiki/Democratic_Party_of_the_Left "Democratic Party of the Left"), a [democratic socialist](/wiki/Democratic_socialist "Democratic socialist") and [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democratic "Social democratic") party,[[62]](#cite_note-Busky2002-62)[[63]](#cite_note-Giugni2004-63)[[64]](#cite_note-Louis2011-64) which is considered the [post-Communist](/wiki/Post-Communist "Post-Communist") evolution of the PCI.[[65]](#cite_note-Parker1996-65)[[66]](#cite_note-Samuels2005-66)[[67]](#cite_note-ErssonLane1998-67)

### President of the Chamber of Deputies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: President of the Chamber of Deputies")]

In 1992, Napolitano was elected [president of the Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/President_of_the_Chamber_of_Deputies_(Italy) "President of the Chamber of Deputies (Italy)"), replacing [Oscar Luigi Scalfaro](/wiki/Oscar_Luigi_Scalfaro "Oscar Luigi Scalfaro"), who became president of the Italian Republic.[[26]](#cite_note-McFadden_2023-26) That legislature was hit by *[Tangentopoli](/wiki/Tangentopoli "Tangentopoli")* and his presidency became one of the fronts of the relationship between the judiciary and politics. He was a prominent symbol of the [Second Italian Republic](/wiki/Second_Italian_Republic "Second Italian Republic") that ensued.[[27]](#cite_note-Bincher_2023-27)

### Late 1990s and early 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Late 1990s and early 2000s")]

After the [1996 Italian general election](/wiki/1996_Italian_general_election "1996 Italian general election"), the [Italian centre-left](/wiki/Italian_centre-left "Italian centre-left") Prime Minister [Romano Prodi](/wiki/Romano_Prodi "Romano Prodi") selected him as Minister of the Interior. He was the first former Communist to hold the office, a role traditionally occupied by Christian Democrats.[[26]](#cite_note-McFadden_2023-26) In this capacity, he took part, together with fellow lawmaker and cabinet minister [Livia Turco](/wiki/Livia_Turco "Livia Turco"), in drafting the government-sponsored law on immigration control (Law No. 40 of 6 March 1998),[[68]](#cite_note-68) which is better known as the [Turco–Napolitano Act](/w/index.php?title=Turco%E2%80%93Napolitano_Act&action=edit&redlink=1 "Turco–Napolitano Act (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legge_Turco-Napolitano "it:Legge Turco-Napolitano")].[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) This bill was partially reformed by the [third Berlusconi government](/wiki/Third_Berlusconi_government "Third Berlusconi government") in 2002 with the [Bossi–Fini Act](/w/index.php?title=Bossi%E2%80%93Fini_Act&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bossi–Fini Act (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legge_Bossi-Fini "it:Legge Bossi-Fini")].[[72]](#cite_note-72)

Napolitano remained Minister of the Interior until October 1998, when Prodi's government lost its majority in the [Italian Parliament](/wiki/Italian_Parliament "Italian Parliament"). He also served a second term as a [member of the European Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_the_European_Parliament "Member of the European Parliament") from 1999 to 2004 within the [Party of European Socialists](/wiki/Party_of_European_Socialists "Party of European Socialists"), and was part of the [European Parliament Committee on Constitutional Affairs](/wiki/European_Parliament_Committee_on_Constitutional_Affairs "European Parliament Committee on Constitutional Affairs").[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) In October 2005, Napolitano was named [senator for life](/wiki/Senator_for_life "Senator for life"),[[75]](#cite_note-75) and was one of the last two to be appointed by [Carlo Azeglio Ciampi](/wiki/Carlo_Azeglio_Ciampi "Carlo Azeglio Ciampi"), the then Italian president of the Republic, together with [Sergio Pininfarina](/wiki/Sergio_Pininfarina "Sergio Pininfarina").[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

## President of Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: President of Italy")]

### First term (2006–2013)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: First term (2006–2013)")]

#### Election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Election")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/14/Ciampi_Napolitano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ciampi_Napolitano.jpg)

Napolitano with his predecessor [Carlo Azeglio Ciampi](/wiki/Carlo_Azeglio_Ciampi "Carlo Azeglio Ciampi")

The [2006 Italian general election](/wiki/2006_Italian_general_election "2006 Italian general election") saw a victory of Prodi, Italy's centre-left coalition candidate, against the incumbent [Italian centre-right](/wiki/Italian_centre-right "Italian centre-right") Prime Minister [Silvio Berlusconi](/wiki/Silvio_Berlusconi "Silvio Berlusconi"). After the election, the presidents of both houses of Parliament were chosen by the winning centre-left coalition, and so the centre-right [House of Freedoms](/wiki/House_of_Freedoms "House of Freedoms") demanded an impartial candidate for the role of president of the Italian Republic. [The Union](/wiki/The_Union_(Italy) "The Union (Italy)") stressed the fact that the Italian constitution demands that the president be a defender of the constitution, hinting that such a quality was scarce among the opposition members. Berlusconi complained about Napolitano's over fifty-year Communist militancy, reiterated his allegations of election fraud, and said that "there is a fake majority that with a 24 thousand vote difference in just one month has occupied the government, presidency of the Chamber, the Senate, and the Republic!"[[78]](#cite_note-La_Stampa_2006-78)

In line with the anti-communist stance he had taken in the campaign, Berlusconi was the most vocal opponent of any candidate that came from the PCI. His allies, especially the [Union of Christian and Centre Democrats](/wiki/Union_of_Christian_and_Centre_Democrats "Union of Christian and Centre Democrats") (UDC), openly disagreed with his intransigence but vowed to stick with their ally's decision. When Napolitano was elected, Berlusconi gave an interview to *[Panorama](/wiki/Panorama_(magazine) "Panorama (magazine)")*, one of his political magazines, saying that the UDC betrayed him by letting 60 of his electors cast a blank vote on the first ballot, instead of supporting the official candidate [Gianni Letta](/wiki/Gianni_Letta "Gianni Letta"). When the UDC argued that this might have spelt the end of the coalition, Berlusconi changed his stance by saying that he had been misunderstood and that he never gave that journalist an interview.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

The candidacy of [Massimo D'Alema](/wiki/Massimo_D%27Alema "Massimo D'Alema") was supported by Napolitano's party, the [Democrats of the Left](/wiki/Democrats_of_the_Left "Democrats of the Left"), and by other parties of the coalition, such as the [Party of Italian Communists](/wiki/Party_of_Italian_Communists "Party of Italian Communists"), the [Communist Refoundation Party](/wiki/Communist_Refoundation_Party "Communist Refoundation Party"), and [Democracy is Freedom – The Daisy](/wiki/Democracy_is_Freedom_%E2%80%93_The_Daisy "Democracy is Freedom – The Daisy"); it was opposed by others, such as the [Rose in the Fist](/wiki/Rose_in_the_Fist "Rose in the Fist"), arguing that his candidacy was driven by a [particracy](/wiki/Particracy "Particracy")'s mentality. Part of the more left-wing coalition considered D'Alema far too willing to conduct backroom deals with the opposition. Some moderate journalists liked D'Alema because his presidency would have given Prodi a stable government since the biggest party of the Union had not been rewarded with any institutional position. Initially, it was also thought that the incumbent president [Carlo Azeglio Ciampi](/wiki/Carlo_Azeglio_Ciampi "Carlo Azeglio Ciampi"), who nominated Napolitano senator for life in 2005, could be re-elected, being favoured by the centre-right coalition over D'Alema; Ciampi refused, and the frontrunner soon became D'Alema.[[80]](#cite_note-ANSA_2015-80)

In the opposition coalition, while Berlusconi vehemently opposed a D'Alema presidency, some of his aides, such as [Mediaset](/wiki/Mediaset "Mediaset") president [Fedele Confalonieri](/wiki/Fedele_Confalonieri "Fedele Confalonieri") and [Marcello Dell'Utri](/wiki/Marcello_Dell%27Utri "Marcello Dell'Utri"), used the electoral fraud allegations as a means to get a president more favourable to Berlusconi saying that they were willing to move on from the electoral fraud allegations, and some right-leaning newspapers, such as *[Il Foglio](/wiki/Il_Foglio "Il Foglio")*, campaigned for D'Alema. The official stance of the centre-right was that D'Alema, being an important left-wing politician and having participated in the election campaign, was ill-suited for president, a role that it is supposed to be impartial. When the Union proposed Napolitano, the House of Freedom objected that the Union should have presented a list of names. Even though Napolitano appeared at first a candidate that the House of Freedoms could converge on, the proposal was rejected much like that of D'Alema. On 5 May, Berlusconi had been unable to convince [Gianfranco Fini](/wiki/Gianfranco_Fini "Gianfranco Fini") of [National Alliance](/wiki/National_Alliance_(Italy) "National Alliance (Italy)") to support D'Alema, while the Democrats of the Left explored the candidacy of [Giuliano Amato](/wiki/Giuliano_Amato "Giuliano Amato") and [Emma Bonino](/wiki/Emma_Bonino "Emma Bonino") before [Piero Fassino](/wiki/Piero_Fassino "Piero Fassino") and D'Alema, in agreement with Prodi, shifted to then 81-year-old Napolitano.[[80]](#cite_note-ANSA_2015-80)

On 7 May, the centre-left majority coalition officially endorsed Napolitano as its candidate in the [2006 Italian presidential election](/wiki/2006_Italian_presidential_election "2006 Italian presidential election"), which began on 8 May. The [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") endorsed him as president through its official newspaper, *[L'Osservatore Romano](/wiki/L%27Osservatore_Romano "L'Osservatore Romano")*, just after the Union named him as its candidate, as did [Marco Follini](/wiki/Marco_Follini "Marco Follini"), former secretary of the UDC, a member party of the House of Freedoms. Napolitano was elected on 10 May, in the fourth round of voting — the first of those requiring only an absolute majority, unlike the first three which required two-thirds of the votes — with 543 votes (out of a possible 1,009).[[81]](#cite_note-81) At the age of 80, he became the first former Communist to become president of Italy, as well as the third Neapolitan after [Enrico De Nicola](/wiki/Enrico_De_Nicola "Enrico De Nicola") and [Giovanni Leone](/wiki/Giovanni_Leone "Giovanni Leone"). He came out of retirement to accept.[[58]](#cite_note-mahalo.com-58)

After Napolitano's election, expressions of esteem toward him personally as regarding his authoritative character as the future president of the Italian Republic were made by both members of the Union and of the House of Freedoms, which had turned in [blank votes](/wiki/Blank_vote "Blank vote"), such as [Pier Ferdinando Casini](/wiki/Pier_Ferdinando_Casini "Pier Ferdinando Casini").[[82]](#cite_note-82) Nevertheless, some Italian right-wing newspapers, such as *[il Giornale](/wiki/Il_Giornale "Il Giornale")*, expressed concerns about his Communist past.[[83]](#cite_note-83) He started his term on 15 May. After his election, Berlusconi commented: "Best wishes to Napolitano, nothing to complain about the person, but now I hope that he will carry out his role with true impartiality."[[78]](#cite_note-La_Stampa_2006-78) Over the years, Berlusconi changed his mind about Napolitano until he refused to pardon him for his conviction in the [Mediaset trial](/wiki/Mediaset_trial_(2005%E2%80%932013) "Mediaset trial (2005–2013)").[[80]](#cite_note-ANSA_2015-80)

#### Prodi Cabinet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Prodi Cabinet")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Prodi_Napolitano.jpg/250px-Prodi_Napolitano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prodi_Napolitano.jpg)

Napolitano with [Romano Prodi](/wiki/Romano_Prodi "Romano Prodi") in 2006

On 9 July 2006, Napolitano was present at the [2006 FIFA World Cup final](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup_final "2006 FIFA World Cup final"), in which the [Italy national team](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team") defeated France and won its fourth World Cup, and afterwards he joined the players' celebrations. He is the second president of the Italian Republic to be present at a FIFA World Cup final won by the Italian team after [Sandro Pertini](/wiki/Sandro_Pertini "Sandro Pertini") in 1982.[[84]](#cite_note-84) On 26 September 2006, Napolitano made an official visit to [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"), Hungary, where he paid tribute to the fallen in the 1956 revolution, which he initially opposed as member of the PCI, by laying a wreath at [Imre Nagy](/wiki/Imre_Nagy "Imre Nagy")'s grave.[[85]](#cite_note-85) On 10 February 2007, a diplomatic crisis arose between Italy and [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") after Napolitano made an official speech during the celebration of the [National Memorial Day of the Exiles and Foibe](/wiki/National_Memorial_Day_of_the_Exiles_and_Foibe "National Memorial Day of the Exiles and Foibe"). In the speech, he stated:

> Already in the unleashing of the first wave of blind and extreme violence in those lands, in the autumn of 1943, summary and tumultuous justicialism, nationalist paroxysm, social retaliation and a plan to eradicate Italian presence intertwined in what was, and ceased to be, the Julian March. There was, therefore, a movement of hate and bloodthirsty fury, and a Slavic annexationist design, which prevailed above all in the peace treaty of 1947, and assumed the sinister shape of "ethnic cleansing". What we can say for sure is that what was consumed – in the most evident way through the inhuman ferocity of the *foibe* – was one of the barbarities of the past century.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Bush_and_Napolitano.jpg/250px-Bush_and_Napolitano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bush_and_Napolitano.jpg)

Napolitano with United States president [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") in 2007

The [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") did not comment on this event but partly condemned the response by Croatian president [Stjepan Mesić](/wiki/Stjepan_Mesi%C4%87 "Stjepan Mesić"), who described Napolitano's statement as racist because Napolitano did not refer to either Slovenians or Croatians as a nation when he spoke about a "Slavic annexationist design" for the [Julian March](/wiki/Julian_March "Julian March");[[87]](#cite_note-87) at the time, Slovenians and Croatians fought together with the [Yugoslav Partisans](/wiki/Yugoslav_Partisans "Yugoslav Partisans"). Another matter of debate in Croatia was that Napolitano made awards to relatives of 25 foibe victims, who included the last fascist Italian [prefect](/wiki/Prefect "Prefect") in [Zadar](/wiki/Zadar "Zadar"), [Vincenzo Serrentino](/wiki/Vincenzo_Serrentino "Vincenzo Serrentino"), who was sentenced to death in 1947 in [Šibenik](/wiki/%C5%A0ibenik "Šibenik").[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89) That was seen by Mesić as historical revisionism and open support for [revanchism](/wiki/Revanchism "Revanchism"). Napolitano's remarks on the foibe massacres were praised by both centre-left and centre-right in Italy, and both coalitions condemned Mesić's statements, while the whole of Croatia stood by Mesić, who later acknowledged that Napolitano did not want to put in discussion the [Peace Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_peace_with_Italy_(1947) "Treaty of peace with Italy (1947)") of 1947, saying that it is "absolutely unacceptable".[[90]](#cite_note-90)

On 21 February 2007, Prodi submitted his resignation after losing a foreign policy vote in Parliament;[[91]](#cite_note-91) Napolitano held talks with the political groups in Parliament, and rejected the resignation on 24 February,[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93) prompting Prodi to ask for a new [vote of confidence](/wiki/Vote_of_confidence "Vote of confidence").[[94]](#cite_note-94) Prodi's cabinet remained in office after he won the vote in the upper house on 28 February,[[95]](#cite_note-Italian_PM_survives_Senate_vote-95)[[96]](#cite_note-Italian_PM_survives_House_vote-96) and then in the lower house on 2 March.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

#### 2008 political crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2008 political crisis")]

Main article: [2008 Italian political crisis](/wiki/2008_Italian_political_crisis "2008 Italian political crisis")

On 24 January 2008, Prodi lost a vote of confidence in the [Senate of the Republic](/wiki/Senate_of_the_Republic_(Italy) "Senate of the Republic (Italy)") by a vote of 161 to 156 votes, after the [Union of Democrats for Europe](/wiki/Union_of_Democrats_for_Europe "Union of Democrats for Europe") ended its support for the Prodi-led government.[[98]](#cite_note-BBC_loses-98) On 30 January, Napolitano appointed the president of the Senate [Franco Marini](/wiki/Franco_Marini "Franco Marini") to try to form a caretaker government with the goal of changing the current electoral system rather than call a quick election.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Berlusconi_Napolitano.jpg/250px-Berlusconi_Napolitano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Berlusconi_Napolitano.jpg)

Napolitano with [Silvio Berlusconi](/wiki/Silvio_Berlusconi "Silvio Berlusconi") in 2008

The state of the [Italian electoral law of 2005](/wiki/Italian_electoral_law_of_2005 "Italian electoral law of 2005") had been under criticism not only within the outgoing government but also among the opposition and in the general population because of the impossibility to choose candidates directly and of the risks that a close-call election might not grant a stable majority in the Senate. After Marini was given the mandate, [Bruno Tabacci](/wiki/Bruno_Tabacci "Bruno Tabacci") and [Mario Baccini](/wiki/Mario_Baccini "Mario Baccini") splintered from the [Union of Christian and Centre Democrats](/wiki/Union_of_Christian_and_Centre_Democrats "Union of Christian and Centre Democrats") to form the [White Rose](/wiki/White_Rose_(Italy) "White Rose (Italy)"), while [Ferdinando Adornato](/wiki/Ferdinando_Adornato "Ferdinando Adornato") and [Angelo Sanza](/w/index.php?title=Angelo_Sanza&action=edit&redlink=1 "Angelo Sanza (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angelo_Sanza "it:Angelo Sanza")] (two leading members of the [Forza Italia](/wiki/Forza_Italia_(1994) "Forza Italia (1994)") faction Liberal-Popular Union) switched allegiance to the UDC. On 4 February, the [Liberal Populars](/wiki/Liberal_Populars "Liberal Populars") (a UDC faction that favoured merging with Forza Italia) seceded from UDC to join Berlusconi's [People of Freedom](/wiki/The_People_of_Freedom "The People of Freedom") later this year. That same day, Marini acknowledged that he had failed to find the necessary majority for an interim government.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

After having met with all major political forces and having found opposition to forming an interim government mainly from centre-right coalition parties, such as Forza Italia and [National Alliance](/wiki/National_Alliance_(Italy) "National Alliance (Italy)"), that were favoured in a possible next election and strongly in favour of an early vote,[[101]](#cite_note-101) Marini resigned his mandate.[[102]](#cite_note-102) Napolitano summoned [Fausto Bertinotti](/wiki/Fausto_Bertinotti "Fausto Bertinotti") and Marini, the two speakers of the houses of Parliament, acknowledging the end of the legislature on 5 February.[[103]](#cite_note-ANSA_2008-103) He [dissolved the Italian Parliament](/wiki/Dissolved_the_Italian_Parliament "Dissolved the Italian Parliament") on 6 February. The ensuing [snap election](/wiki/Snap_election "Snap election") was held on 13 and 14 April 2008,[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) together with the [2008 Italian local elections](/w/index.php?title=2008_Italian_local_elections&action=edit&redlink=1 "2008 Italian local elections (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elezioni_amministrative_in_Italia_del_2008 "it:Elezioni amministrative in Italia del 2008")].[[103]](#cite_note-ANSA_2008-103) The elections resulted in a decisive victory for Berlusconi's centre-right coalition.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

On 7 May, Napolitano appointed Berlusconi as [Prime Minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy") following his [landslide victory](/wiki/Landslide_victory "Landslide victory") in the [2008 Italian general election](/wiki/2008_Italian_general_election "2008 Italian general election"). The cabinet was officially inaugurated one day later, with Berlusconi thus becoming the second of five prime ministers in nine years under Napolitano. During the ensuing Berlusconi
government, Napolitano was at times criticized by the parliamentary opposition for having signed some of the laws approved by Parliament on the government's proposal, which were view critically by part of the opposition, particularly [Antonio Di Pietro](/wiki/Antonio_Di_Pietro "Antonio Di Pietro"),[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109) who evaluated the possibility of an [impeachment](/wiki/Impeachment "Impeachment") motion of Napolitano after he had signed a government decree-law for the readmission of the excluded Berlusconi's party lists in Lazio and Lombardy a few weeks before the [2010 Italian regional elections](/wiki/2010_Italian_regional_elections "2010 Italian regional elections").[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111)

#### Eluana Englaro incident

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Eluana Englaro incident")]

On 6 February 2009, Napolitano refused to sign an emergency decree made by the Berlusconi government in order to suspend a final court sentence allowing suspension of nutrition to 38-year-old coma patient [Eluana Englaro](/wiki/Eluana_Englaro "Eluana Englaro"); the decree could not be enacted by Berlusconi. This caused a major political debate within Italy regarding the relationship between Napolitano and the government in office.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

#### 2011 political crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2011 political crisis")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Monti_Merkel_Napolitano.jpg/250px-Monti_Merkel_Napolitano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monti_Merkel_Napolitano.jpg)

Napolitano with [Mario Monti](/wiki/Mario_Monti "Mario Monti") and the then German chancellor [Angela Merkel](/wiki/Angela_Merkel "Angela Merkel")

In July 2011, Napolitano was Italy's most popular politician, with an 80% popularity rating compared to Berlusconi's 30% rating as prime minister. Citing [Giuseppe Mazzini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Mazzini "Giuseppe Mazzini")'s "We Have No Flag" speech of 1844, he appealed to the [regions of Italy](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy") to resolve the country's crisis.[[113]](#cite_note-113) On 11 October, the Chamber of Deputies rejected the law on the budget of the state proposed by the government.[[114]](#cite_note-114) As a result of this event, Berlusconi moved for a confidence vote in the Chamber of Deputies on 14 October; he won the vote with just 316 votes to 310, the minimum required to retain a majority.[[115]](#cite_note-115) An increasing number of deputies continued to [cross the floor](/wiki/Cross_the_floor "Cross the floor") and join the opposition. On 8 November, the Chamber of Deputies approved the law on the budget of the state that was previously rejected but with only 308 votes, while opposition parties did not participate in the vote to highlight that Berlusconi had lost his majority.[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117) After the vote, Berlusconi announced his resignation after Parliament passed economic reforms,[[118]](#cite_note-118) as the [bid–ask spread](/wiki/Bid%E2%80%93ask_spread "Bid–ask spread") almost reached 600 points.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

On 12 November, after a final meeting with his cabinet, Berlusconi met Napolitano at the [Quirinal Palace](/wiki/Quirinal_Palace "Quirinal Palace") to tend his resignation, which Napolitano accepted,[[120]](#cite_note-120) in what was described as an end of an era, Berlusconi being Italy's longest-serving post-war prime minister,[[121]](#cite_note-121) and his 17-year premiership in total marred by many scandals.[[122]](#cite_note-122) This event was met with jeering and celebrations.[[123]](#cite_note-123) As Berlusconi arrived at the presidential residence, a hostile crowd gathered with banners shouting insults at Berlusconi and throwing coins at the car. After his resignation, the booing and jeering continued as he left in his convoy, with the public shouting words like *buffoon*, *dictator*, and *[mafioso](/wiki/Sicilian_Mafia "Sicilian Mafia")*.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

Following [Berlusconi's resignation](/wiki/Berlusconi%27s_resignation "Berlusconi's resignation"), Napolitano decided to appoint former European commissioner [Mario Monti](/wiki/Mario_Monti "Mario Monti") as a senator for life, and then as Prime Minister-designate. The [Monti Cabinet](/wiki/Monti_Cabinet "Monti Cabinet") was subsequently confirmed by an overwhelming majority of both houses of Parliament in what was a [technocratic cabinet](/wiki/Technocratic_government_(Italy) "Technocratic government (Italy)"),[[125]](#cite_note-125) which was widely referred to as a "government of the president". He also earned the nickname "King George" (*re Giorgio*), which *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* attributed to "his stately defense of Italian democratic institutions".[[3]](#cite_note-Donadio_2011-3)[[126]](#cite_note-126) Napolitano's management of the events led to widespread international attention in his capacity as president, a role normally regarded as largely ceremonial. He received complimentary calls from multiple world leaders,[[26]](#cite_note-McFadden_2023-26) including by the United States under [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), with whom he had positive relations.[[127]](#cite_note-127) In December 2011, *[L'Espresso](/wiki/L%27Espresso "L'Espresso")* declared 2011 to be the year of Napolitano and named him the man of the year.[[128]](#cite_note-128) While also used affectionately, the "King George" moniker was seen negatively by his critics on the Italian right, as they alleged that he had helped engineer the end of Berlusconi's final government after colluding with European Union authorities.[[129]](#cite_note-129)

Monti later said that, due to Napolitano's role, he had achieved the largest parliamentary majority and argued that he did not fuel populism. He said that in 2013 he was implored by the governing parties to remain as prime minister.[[130]](#cite_note-130) Critics accused Napolitano of orchestrating a coup against Berlusconi, and that he did this to help his former comrades; in fact, many people in the Quirinale told Casini that Berlusconi himself felt relieved, Berlusconi's party was instrumental to the Monti government formation, and Berlusconi himself supported Napolitano's re-election in 2013. According to Casini, the coup story helped the centre-right coalition's electoral campaign, with the centre-left coalition suffering the most for their support of Monti even though they had everything to gain from new elections.[[131]](#cite_note-131) At Napolitano's funeral in 2023, Gianni Letta dismissed the [conspiracy theories](/wiki/Conspiracy_theories "Conspiracy theories") related to the alleged 2011 coup against Belusconi.[[132]](#cite_note-132)

### Second term (2013–2015)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Second term (2013–2015)")]

#### Re-election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Re-election")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Boldrini%2C_Napolitano_and_Grasso_2013_-_2.jpg/250px-Boldrini%2C_Napolitano_and_Grasso_2013_-_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boldrini,_Napolitano_and_Grasso_2013_-_2.jpg)

Napolitano with [Laura Boldrini](/wiki/Laura_Boldrini "Laura Boldrini") and [Pietro Grasso](/wiki/Pietro_Grasso "Pietro Grasso")

In February 2013, Napolitano wrote for *L'Osservatore Romano*. About the [Fall of Communism](/wiki/Fall_of_Communism "Fall of Communism"), he spoke of the "overthrow of that revolutionary utopia which contained within itself promises of social emancipation and human liberation and which had ended up — as [Norberto Bobbio](/wiki/Norberto_Bobbio "Norberto Bobbio") said with a withering expression — by being overturned, in the conversion of done in its opposite."[[133]](#cite_note-La_Stampa_2013-133) At the same time, he said that "the [conservative ideology](/wiki/Conservative_ideology "Conservative ideology") survived the end of Communism, increasingly taking on the appearance of that '[market fundamentalism](/wiki/Market_fundamentalism "Market fundamentalism")', translated into [deregulation](/wiki/Deregulation "Deregulation") and the abdication of politics, which only the global financial crisis which broke out in 2008 would have called into question."[[133]](#cite_note-La_Stampa_2013-133)

Following five inconclusive ballots for the [April 2013 Italian presidential election](/wiki/April_2013_Italian_presidential_election "April 2013 Italian presidential election"), Napolitano accepted to be re-elected as president – an unprecedented move – following pleas by Monti and the leaders of the main political blocks, [Pier Luigi Bersani](/wiki/Pier_Luigi_Bersani "Pier Luigi Bersani") and Berlusconi.[[134]](#cite_note-134) Napolitano reluctantly agreed to serve for another term in order to safeguard the continuity of the country's institutions.[[16]](#cite_note-Mackenzie_2013-16)[[135]](#cite_note-135) Napolitano was easily re-elected on 20 April, receiving 738 of the 1,007 possible votes, and was sworn in on 22 April after a speech when he asked for constitutional and electoral reforms.[[136]](#cite_note-136) The critical findings on *Porcellum* (the Italian electoral law of 2005) echoed the words that Napolitano gave on 22 April before the Electoral College that had re-elected him for a second term.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

#### Letta Cabinet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Letta Cabinet")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Letta_Napolitano.jpg/250px-Letta_Napolitano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Letta_Napolitano.jpg)

Napolitano with [Enrico Letta](/wiki/Enrico_Letta "Enrico Letta") in 2013

After his re-election, Napolitano immediately began consultations with the chairmen of the Chamber of Deputies, Senate, and political forces after the failure of the previous attempt with Bersani after the inconclusive [February 2013 Italian general election](/wiki/February_2013_Italian_general_election "February 2013 Italian general election"), and the establishment of a panel of ten experts by Napolitano himself, who were dubbed as wise men by the press,[[138]](#cite_note-138)[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[140]](#cite_note-140) in order to outline priorities and formulate an agenda to deal with the persistent economic hardship and growing unemployment and end the country's political deadlock,[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-142)[[143]](#cite_note-143) as well as to nominate his successor.[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Queen_Elizabeth_II_was_greeted_by_Giorgio_Napolitano_during_the_official_state_visit_%289%29.jpg/250px-Queen_Elizabeth_II_was_greeted_by_Giorgio_Napolitano_during_the_official_state_visit_%289%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Elizabeth_II_was_greeted_by_Giorgio_Napolitano_during_the_official_state_visit_(9).jpg)

Napolitano with [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II") in 2014

On 24 April, Napolitano gave to the vice-secretary of the centre-left [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(Italy) "Democratic Party (Italy)"), [Enrico Letta](/wiki/Enrico_Letta "Enrico Letta"), the task of forming a government,[[146]](#cite_note-146) having determined that Bersani, leader of the winning coalition [Italy Common Good](/wiki/Italy_Common_Good "Italy Common Good"), could not form a government because it did not have a majority in the Senate.[[147]](#cite_note-Frye_2013-147) Bersani refused a coalition with Berlusconi's party and attempted to form a government with the anti-establishment [Five Star Movement](/wiki/Five_Star_Movement "Five Star Movement") but failed in his bid.[[148]](#cite_note-148)

On 27 April, Letta formally accepted the task of leading a [grand coalition](/wiki/Grand_coalition "Grand coalition") government, which was described as unprecedened, with support from the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(Italy) "Democratic Party (Italy)"), of which he remained deputy secretary, the centre-right [People of Freedom](/wiki/People_of_Freedom "People of Freedom") of Berlusconi, and the centrist [Civic Choice](/wiki/Civic_Choice "Civic Choice") of Monti, and subsequently listed the members of the [Letta Cabinet](/wiki/Letta_Cabinet "Letta Cabinet"). Napolitano described it as the "only government possible",[[149]](#cite_note-149) and said: "I hope that this government can get to work quickly in the spirit of fervent co-operation and without any prejudice or conflict. It was and is the only possible government."[[150]](#cite_note-BBC_2013-150)

The government formed by Letta became the first in the history of the Italian Republic to include representatives of all the major candidate-coalitions that had competed in the election, and included a record number of women.[[151]](#cite_note-151) His close relationship with his uncle Gianni Letta, one of Berlusconi's most trusted advisors, was perceived as a way of overcoming the bitter hostility between the two opposing camps.[[152]](#cite_note-Winfield2013-152)[[153]](#cite_note-TheAustralian2013-153) Letta appointed [Angelino Alfano](/wiki/Angelino_Alfano "Angelino Alfano"), secretary of the People of Freedom, as his [Deputy Prime Minister of Italy](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Deputy Prime Minister of Italy"). Letta was formally sworn in as prime minister on 28 April; during the ceremony, a man fired shots outside [Palazzo Chigi](/wiki/Palazzo_Chigi "Palazzo Chigi") and wounded two [Carabinieri](/wiki/Carabinieri "Carabinieri").[[147]](#cite_note-Frye_2013-147)[[150]](#cite_note-BBC_2013-150)

On 30 January 2014, the Five Star Movement deposited an impeachment motion,[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[155]](#cite_note-155) which was criticized by the other parties and was later dismissed by the impeachment committee,[[156]](#cite_note-156) for six charges accusing Napolitano of harming the Italian constitution,[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159) to allow unconstitutional laws and in relation to the [State-Mafia Pact](/wiki/State-Mafia_Pact "State-Mafia Pact"),[[160]](#cite_note-160) which remained controversial after his death in 2023.[[161]](#cite_note-161)[[162]](#cite_note-162)[[163]](#cite_note-163)

#### Renzi Cabinet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Renzi Cabinet")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Renzi_Napolitano.jpg/250px-Renzi_Napolitano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Renzi_Napolitano.jpg)

Napolitano with [Matteo Renzi](/wiki/Matteo_Renzi "Matteo Renzi") in 2014

In the [2013 Democratic Party leadership election](/wiki/2013_Democratic_Party_(Italy)_leadership_election "2013 Democratic Party (Italy) leadership election"), [Matteo Renzi](/wiki/Matteo_Renzi "Matteo Renzi"), the mayor of [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), was elected with 68% of the popular vote, compared to 18% for [Gianni Cuperlo](/wiki/Gianni_Cuperlo "Gianni Cuperlo"), and 14% for [Giuseppe Civati](/wiki/Giuseppe_Civati "Giuseppe Civati"). He became the new secretary of the Democratic Party and the centre-left's prospective candidate for Prime Minister. His victory was welcomed by Letta, who had been the vice-secretary of the party under Bersani's leadership. In an earlier speech, Renzi had paid tribute to Letta, saying that he was not intended to put him "on trial". Without directly proposing himself as the next Prime Minister, he said the Eurozone's third-largest economy urgently needed "a new phase" and "radical programme" to push through badly needed reforms. The motion he put forward made clear "the necessity and urgency of opening a new phase with a new executive". Speaking privately to party leaders, Renzi said that Italy was "at a crossroads" and faced either holding fresh elections or a new government without a return to the polls.[[164]](#cite_note-164)

On 13 February, Letta dismissed rumours of his resignation.[[165]](#cite_note-165) On 14 February, Napolitano accepted Letta's resignation from the office of Prime Minister;[[166]](#cite_note-166) Napolitano wanted to avoid new elections until changes were made to Italy's electoral law, which many blamed for producing the deadlock and stalemate of Italian politics during those years;[[167]](#cite_note-167) he said that he wanted to resolve the political crisis "as quickly as possible" so that a new government in what he described as "this fragile economic phase" could pass a new electoral law and institutional reforms.[[168]](#cite_note-168) Following Letta's resignation, Renzi formally received the task of forming the new Italian government from Napolitano on 17 February.[[169]](#cite_note-169) Renzi held several days of talks with party leaders, all of which he broadcast live on the internet, before unveiling his Cabinet on 21 February, which contained members of the Democratic Party, the [New Centre-Right](/wiki/New_Centre-Right "New Centre-Right"), the [Union of the Centre](/wiki/Union_of_the_Centre_(2002) "Union of the Centre (2002)"), and the Civic Choice. His cabinet became Italy's youngest government to date, with an average age of 47.[[170]](#cite_note-170) It was also the first in which the number of female ministers was equal to the number of male ministers, excluding the Prime Minister.[[171]](#cite_note-171) On 22 February, Renzi was formally sworn in as prime minister, becoming the fourth prime minister in four years and the youngest prime minister in the [history of Italy](/wiki/History_of_Italy "History of Italy"), and the [Renzi Cabinet](/wiki/Renzi_Cabinet "Renzi Cabinet") was established.[[172]](#cite_note-172)

### Resignation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Resignation")]

On 9 November 2014, the Italian press reported that Napolitano would step down at the end of the year.[[173]](#cite_note-173) The press office of the Quirinal Palace "neither confirmed nor denied" the reports.[[174]](#cite_note-174) Citing age reasons,[[175]](#cite_note-Kirchgaessner_2015-175) Napolitano officially resigned on 14 January 2015 after the end of the six-month Italian presidency of the European Union.[[176]](#cite_note-176)

### Later life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Later life")]

After the presidency, Napolitano once again became [senator for life in Italy](/wiki/Senator_for_life_in_Italy "Senator for life in Italy") after the end of his presidency on 14 January 2015. On 19 January, he joined the [For the Autonomies](/wiki/For_the_Autonomies "For the Autonomies") group in the Senate. On 23 and 24 March 2018, he was the provisional president of the Senate as the most senior senator during the election of the new president of the Senate in the [18th legislature of the Italian Republic](/wiki/18th_legislature_of_the_Italian_Republic "18th legislature of the Italian Republic").[[177]](#cite_note-177)

Napolitano had surgery on his [aorta](/wiki/Aorta "Aorta") in April 2018, and was in the [intensive care unit](/wiki/Intensive_care_unit "Intensive care unit") after abdominal surgery in May 2021. On 13 October 2022, on the occasion of the first session of the [19th legislature of the Italian Republic](/wiki/19th_legislature_of_the_Italian_Republic "19th legislature of the Italian Republic"), he renounced to the role of provisional president of the Senate, which was taken by [Liliana Segre](/wiki/Liliana_Segre "Liliana Segre").[[178]](#cite_note-178)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Personal life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Abdullah_Gul_and_Giorgio_Napolitano_3.jpg/250px-Abdullah_Gul_and_Giorgio_Napolitano_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abdullah_Gul_and_Giorgio_Napolitano_3.jpg)

Napolitano and his wife [Clio Maria Bittoni](/wiki/Clio_Maria_Bittoni "Clio Maria Bittoni") with Turkish president [Abdullah Gül](/wiki/Abdullah_G%C3%BCl "Abdullah Gül") and his wife in 2009

At the beginning of 1959, Napolitano met [Clio Maria Bittoni](/wiki/Clio_Maria_Bittoni "Clio Maria Bittoni") (1934–2024), who had graduated in law. They were married at the town hall in Rome a few months later in a civil ceremony. From the marriage were born Giovanni (born 1961), who has two children named Sofia and Simone,[[179]](#cite_note-179) and Giulio (born 1969).[[180]](#cite_note-180)

### Relations with the Catholic Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Relations with the Catholic Church")]

Napolitano was a *laico* (secular layman) and *non credente* (non-believer), effectively an [atheist](/wiki/Atheist "Atheist").[[181]](#cite_note-181) According to the Italian prelate of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and biblical scholar [Gianfranco Ravasi](/wiki/Gianfranco_Ravasi "Gianfranco Ravasi"), Napolitano always rejected the definition of either atheist or [agnostic](/wiki/Agnostic "Agnostic"), which he did not like as formulas, and said that he was a non-believer, a layman who maintained close and positive relations with the Catholic Church and [Pope Benedict XVI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI "Pope Benedict XVI"). During one of their first meetings, Napolitano quoted from [Thomas Mann](/wiki/Thomas_Mann "Thomas Mann"), one of his favourite writers, that "Christianity remains one of the pillars of the Western spirit and the other is the ancient Mediterranean culture."[[182]](#cite_note-182)

Napolitano was the first to know about the pope's resignation. [Andrea Riccardi](/wiki/Andrea_Riccardi "Andrea Riccardi"), founder of the [Community of Sant'Egidio](/wiki/Community_of_Sant%27Egidio "Community of Sant'Egidio") and former minister in the Monti Cabinet, said that "Napolitano considered the Church a component of great importance in the social stability of the country" and was concerned by the rightward shift of Catholics.[[183]](#cite_note-183)

### Death and state funeral

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Death and state funeral")]

Main article: [Death and state funeral of Giorgio Napolitano](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Giorgio_Napolitano "Death and state funeral of Giorgio Napolitano")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Funerale_di_Giorgio_Napolitano.jpg/250px-Funerale_di_Giorgio_Napolitano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Funerale_di_Giorgio_Napolitano.jpg)

Napolitano lying in state before the funeral

In 2023, Napolitano was hospitalized in Rome shortly after his 98th birthday on 29 June.[[184]](#cite_note-184) On 19 September, he was reported to be in [critical condition](/wiki/Critical_condition "Critical condition") with his health deteriorating, and was taken off life support.[[185]](#cite_note-185) He died three days later on 22 September at the age of 98.[[26]](#cite_note-McFadden_2023-26)[[12]](#cite_note-Vecchio_2023-12) Italy's [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_(Italy) "Council of Ministers (Italy)") proclaimed a day of national mourning and ordered flags to be flown [half-mast](/wiki/Half-mast "Half-mast") for five days in his honour.[[186]](#cite_note-186)

Napolitano's [state funeral](/wiki/State_funeral "State funeral") was held on 26 September,[[187]](#cite_note-187) and was attended by international figures including four incumbent presidents, one former president, and over one hundred ambassadors.[[188]](#cite_note-188) In line with his personal views, it was a [secular funeral](/wiki/Secular_funeral "Secular funeral");[[189]](#cite_note-189)[[190]](#cite_note-190)[[191]](#cite_note-191) it was Italy's first funeral for a former president to be held in this form.[[192]](#cite_note-192) He was buried in Rome's [Cimitero Acattolico](/wiki/Cimitero_Acattolico "Cimitero Acattolico"), near other historical figures like [Antonio Gramsci](/wiki/Antonio_Gramsci "Antonio Gramsci"), [Andrea Camilleri](/wiki/Andrea_Camilleri "Andrea Camilleri"), [Emilio Lussu](/wiki/Emilio_Lussu "Emilio Lussu"), [Lindsay Kemp](/wiki/Lindsay_Kemp "Lindsay Kemp"), [Amelia Rosselli](/wiki/Amelia_Rosselli "Amelia Rosselli"), [John Keats](/wiki/John_Keats "John Keats"), and [Percy Bysshe Shelley](/wiki/Percy_Bysshe_Shelley "Percy Bysshe Shelley").[[193]](#cite_note-193)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Napolitano is considered one of the most important figures of the Italian Republic, particularly of the Second Italian Republic between the 1990s and 2010s, and is described as a giant of Italian politics.[[194]](#cite_note-194) He was the first Italian president to be re-elected, and was the first former Communist to achieve the Italian Republic's highest office. His life and career went almost through a century, being the longest-serving and longest-lived president in the history of the Italian Republic. He underwent several changes, from supporting [Soviet Communism](/wiki/Soviet_Communism "Soviet Communism") from the 1940s to the 1960s, from the moderate transition in the 1960s and 1980s, and a man of the institutions from the 1990s to the 2010s.[[195]](#cite_note-195) He is considered to have been a social-democratic, pro-European, and reformist Communist;[[196]](#cite_note-196) *[Le Monde](/wiki/Le_Monde "Le Monde")* described him as "a reformist Communist, capable of dialogue with the leaders of the Christian Democrats and with the trade unions, a convinced pro-European, he participated in many international conferences in Europe, and formed ties with left-wing leaders, such as [Willy Brandt](/wiki/Willy_Brandt "Willy Brandt") in Germany."[[197]](#cite_note-Metro_News_2023-197) [Aldo Tortorella](/wiki/Aldo_Tortorella "Aldo Tortorella"), another long-time Communist member, described him as "a great left-wing fighter".[[198]](#cite_note-198)

*[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* described him as the "Italian post-Communist pillar".[[197]](#cite_note-Metro_News_2023-197) As president of Italy, Napolitano was charged by his critics of having transformed a largely ceremonial role into a political and executive one; he became the *de facto* kingmaker of Italian politics. In 2008 and 2011, he refused to hold snap elections and favoured the formation of new governments instead to carry out reforms. This failed in 2008 when Berlusconi won the ensuing elections, while he succeeded in 2011 when he mandated Monti to form a government,[[199]](#cite_note-199) a decision that remains controversial and polarizing but that it is considered to have saved Italy during the European debt crisis and praised for ending the country's political deadlock.[[26]](#cite_note-McFadden_2023-26) In 2013, he accepted re-election reluctantly;[[16]](#cite_note-Mackenzie_2013-16) before his resignation in 2015 due to age reasons,[[175]](#cite_note-Kirchgaessner_2015-175) he again played a key role in forming Italy's first grand coalition government and ending the political stalemate.[[150]](#cite_note-BBC_2013-150)[[200]](#cite_note-200)[[201]](#cite_note-201)

Napolitano's death attracted international attention and recognition.[[202]](#cite_note-202) The German newspaper *[Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung](/wiki/Frankfurter_Allgemeine_Zeitung "Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung")* described Napolitano as being "highly appreciated at an international level", where he was considered "an impartial and reliable interlocutor",[[197]](#cite_note-Metro_News_2023-197) while [Deutsche Welle](/wiki/Deutsche_Welle "Deutsche Welle") said that he "helped guide Italy through the EU sovereign debt crisis".[[197]](#cite_note-Metro_News_2023-197) In Spain, *[El País](/wiki/El_Pa%C3%ADs "El País")* characterized him as a "convinced European and renowned statesman" who "helped bring his country out of a debt crisis in 2011".[[197]](#cite_note-Metro_News_2023-197) In France, *Le Monde* described him as a "tireless militant" who "played a leading role in Italian political life" and a "symbol of stability and political longevity" during his nine years as head of state, and underscored his evolution from the "Red Prince" to "King George".[[197]](#cite_note-Metro_News_2023-197) In the United States, *The New York Times* emphasized Napolitano's crucial role in stabilizing the country.[[197]](#cite_note-Metro_News_2023-197)

## Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giorgio_Napolitano&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Honours")]

Napolitano received a number of honours, both nationally and internationally. In 2009, he received the *Medaglia Teresiana* at the [University of Pavia](/wiki/University_of_Pavia "University of Pavia").[[203]](#cite_note-203) In 2010, he was awarded the [Dan David Prize](/wiki/Dan_David_Prize "Dan David Prize") "for his dedication to the cause of Parliamentary democracy, thereby contributing to a strengthening of democratic values and institutions in Italy and Europe; and for his courage and intellectual integrity which have been crucial in healing the wounds of the Cold War in Europe, as well as the scars left in Italy in the wake of fascism."[[204]](#cite_note-204)
